coredata.dc:title: Fossicking Pueraria phaseoloides leaf-derived N-doped carbon dot nanofillers engrafted anticorrosive epoxy coatings for mild steel in saline environments
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Paul, Anila
Arshad, Muhammed
Ajayan, Athira
Joseph, Abraham
coredata.dc:description: Leveraging the 'seed to weed' paradigm, a novel azure fluorescent Pueraria phaseoloides (Pp) leaf-derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots (PNCD) with an abundant phytochemical profile was synthesized for marine corrosion abatement. These PNCDs were integrated onto amorphous silica nanoparticles to yield solid silica-loaded PNCD (SPNCD), which were subsequently incorporated into epoxy to produce an enhanced anticorrosive coating. Structural, optical, and morphological assessments substantiated the synthesis and effective adsorption of PNCDs onto nanoscale silica adsorbents. TEM analysis shows that the average diameter of non-toxic PNCD is about 5 .85nm. The excitation at a wavelength of 380 nm of PNCD generated the most intense emission peak at 460 nm. The rate of uniform corrosion on coated mild steel (MS) investigated by gravimetric method. Corrosion performance for MS in saline conditions was evaluated through continuous immersion tests over durations ranging from one hour to sixty days, revealing that the epoxy coating containing 0.4 % SPNCD exhibited exceptional protective efficiency (99 %). The corrosion current density of 0.4ESD is 5 times lesser than that of blank epoxy after 1440 hour of immersion indicating significant enhancement in the anticorrosion property of nanocomposite coating. The long-term immersion studies with 0.4ESD (Qdl=4.71×10–6) coating were performed to compare the durability of bare epoxy (Qdl=1.6 × 10–3) coating. Surface studies were carried out using SEM and AFM before and after corrosion studies which shows that the nanofiller particles can block the diffusion of chloride ions to reduce the corrosion rate.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pueraria phaseoloides
N-doped carbon dot
Epoxy polymer
Nanocomposite, Mild steel, Amorphous silica

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nanofiller and fabrication of coating
Structural and optical characteristics of 0.4ESD film

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the support provided by the University of Calicut for conducting the research at the Department of Chemistry. The research was supported by technical assistance from CSIF-University of Calicut, STIC[sbnd]CUSAT Kerala, DST- PURSE Facility, Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut and KSCSTE Facility at Environmental and Sustainable Chemistry Laboratory (ESCL), Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) structural and stability studies of PNCD under UV-visible light (B) PL Emission Spectra of PNCD (C) fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of PNCD, (D) fluorescence decay profile of PNCD solution, (E) DLS spectra of freshly prepared PNCD, and (F) XRD pattern shows the amorphous nature of PNCD.
(A) TEM image of PNCD particles, (B) HRTEM image showing lattice space in PNCD, (C) histogram depicts the PNCD particle size distribution, and (D) depicts SAED pattern.
(A) FTIR and (B) UV–Visible spectra reveal the presence of PNCD on SPNCD.

(A) Impedance spectra of 1hr immersion study in saline medium, with the inset showing the Nyquist plot of bare metal, (B) Nyquist plot of 0.4 % of ES compared with 0.4 % of ESD through one hour immersion study, (C) Bode impedance plot of 0.4ESD coated MS at different immersion periods, (D) Bode phase angle diagram of 0.4 %ESD coated MS compared with plane epoxy coated MS in two months immersion study, and (E) depicts the electrochemical equivalent circuits used to fit the Nyquist plots.

Tafel plots of (A) different wt% ESD coated MS were studied in aggressive media for one hour (B) 0.4ESD compared with bare, epoxy and epoxy filled silica coated metals, and (C) one month and two month immersion studies of epoxy and nanocomposite coated MS.
The FTIR-ATR spectra of epoxy and 0.4ESD film. The two spectra are distinguished from one another by the magnified portions of the spectra that are provided in additional frameworks.
Solid state optical spectroscopy of 0.4ESD film. The inset shows a picture of the 0.4ESD film exposed to UV and visible light.
The XPS survey spectra of PNCD nano particles and 0.4ESD coated MS specimen. Additionally supplied are high-resolution XPS spectra of the Si2p, C1 s, N1 s, and O1 s of 0.4ESD coated MS sample.

The SEM images of (A) epoxy coated MS, whereby the visible micro pores are highlighted by the red circles, (B) epoxy coated MS after immersion in 3.5wt% NaCl for 24 h, enlarged portion of the image is given in close-up, (C) 0.4ESD coated MS before, and (D) after immersion study. The in-depth view (20 nm) of the nanohybrid coated MS given in between (C) and (D).
The 2D and 3D-AFM images of 0.4ESD coated MS before and after electrochemical corrosion studies. The figure also includes the values for Ra and Rq.
The data from an hour-long immersion research on coated and uncoated metals were fitted using R(Q(R(QR))) and R(QR) circuit respectively.
Nyquist plot parameters of 0.4ESD coated MS fitted with R(Q(R(Q(RW)))) circuit at various immersion times.
PDP results from an hour-long immersion investigation in 3.5wt% NaCl media for coated and uncoated MS samples.
Tafel plot parameters for MS panels coated with epoxy and 0.4ESD were obtained after one and two months of immersion.

head.ce:title: Fossicking Pueraria phaseoloides leaf-derived N-doped carbon dot nanofillers engrafted anticorrosive epoxy coatings for mild steel in saline environments

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut, Calicut University P O, 673 635, Kerala, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut, Calicut University P O, 673 635, Kerala, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Leveraging the 'seed to weed' paradigm, a novel azure fluorescent Pueraria phaseoloides (Pp) leaf-derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots (PNCD) with an abundant phytochemical profile was synthesized for marine corrosion abatement. These PNCDs were integrated onto amorphous silica nanoparticles to yield solid silica-loaded PNCD (SPNCD), which were subsequently incorporated into epoxy to produce an enhanced anticorrosive coating. Structural, optical, and morphological assessments substantiated the synthesis and effective adsorption of PNCDs onto nanoscale silica adsorbents. TEM analysis shows that the average diameter of non-toxic PNCD is about 5 .85nm. The excitation at a wavelength of 380 nm of PNCD generated the most intense emission peak at 460 nm. The rate of uniform corrosion on coated mild steel (MS) investigated by gravimetric method. Corrosion performance for MS in saline conditions was evaluated through continuous immersion tests over durations ranging from one hour to sixty days, revealing that the epoxy coating containing 0.4 % SPNCD exhibited exceptional protective efficiency (99 %). The corrosion current density of 0.4ESD is 5 times lesser than that of blank epoxy after 1440 hour of immersion indicating significant enhancement in the anticorrosion property of nanocomposite coating. The long-term immersion studies with 0.4ESD (Qdl=4.71×10–6) coating were performed to compare the durability of bare epoxy (Qdl=1.6 × 10–3) coating. Surface studies were carried out using SEM and AFM before and after corrosion studies which shows that the nanofiller particles can block the diffusion of chloride ions to reduce the corrosion rate.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Corrosion is a surface-initiated deterioration phenomenon that advances through chemical or electrochemical reactions on the surface of the exposed material. Metals and alloys have become indispensable to our developmental trajectory due to their myriad applications across diverse domains, ranging from rudimentary to advanced industrial sectors. Despite their paramount significance in technical and infrastructural contexts, these materials exhibit a thermodynamic predisposition to succumb to corrosion when exposed to environmental elements, necessitating continued research and innovation in protective strategies to augment their durability and efficacy. In marine environments, metals are extensively employed in desalination plants, construction sites, vessels, aquaculture apparatus, and coastal infrastructure. However, the presence of seawater, replete with NaCl, precipitates corrosion, undermining structural integrity and longevity. While mild steel (MS) offers numerous merits, including cost-effectiveness, durability, and adaptability, its susceptibility to corrosion in saline conditions represents a significant deficiency. This necessitates the pursuit of effective corrosion mitigation strategies, such as bespoke anticorrosive coatings, which may confer a distinct advantage over conventional methodologies like galvanization or cathodic protection. These anticorrosive coatings form a protective barrier on the surface, hindering corrosive agents at the interface, and thereby preventing the onset and progression of corrosion.

Contemporary, eco-friendly green composite coatings are increasingly significant within the domain of anticorrosive coatings [1]. The preponderance of these advanced composite formulations is accomplished by integrating suitable metal-adhesive polymers with nano-solid functional fillers that aggrandize the surface functionality and offer biocompatibility, sensing capabilities, inhibition properties, and cross-linking efficacy [2]. Bibekananda De et al. in situ synthesized carbon dot-infused epoxy nanocomposites, demonstrating their biocompatibility, mechanical robustness, and optical characteristics [3]. Ayesha Kausar elucidated advancements in quantum dot-laden epoxy matrices, examining their applications in light-emitting diodes, detectors, bioimaging, photocatalytic activities, and other domains [4]. In this vein, this piece of research investigates carbon dots (CDs) as nano-solid functional fillers within an epoxy matrix. CDs, spherical carbon entities with diameters under 10 nm, associated with a multitude of functional groups, are distinguished for their multifaceted attributes, hydrophilicity, and fluorescence [5,6]. Recent scrutiny has been directed toward the distinctive properties of green synthesis of CDs employing natural resources, including botanical materials and crab shells [7]. A novel cerium-doped carbon dot exhibiting 97 % inhibitory efficacy at 200 ppm was documented by Zhiji Tang and his research team, employing l-histidine and cerium nitrate hexahydrate [8]. Conversely, Raj Kumar Mehta and associates examined the superior inhibitive properties of heteroatom-doped CDs for MS in 15 % hydrochloric acid solution [9]. Investigations reveal that CDs act as exceptional corrosion inhibitors due to their electron-rich functional moieties, in conjunction with sustainable and environmentally benign attributes that augment the anticorrosive performance of CDs -infused coating matrices [9,10].

The foliage of the nitrogen-fixing legume Pueraria phaseoloides (Roxb.) Benth (Pp), commonly referred to as tropical kudzu and belonging to the Fabaceae family, was selected for this research endeavour to hydrothermally synthesize biocompatible CDs [11,12]. Pp, an ubiquitous weed, has been designated as a high-risk species in the forests of Kerala by the Kerala Forest Research Institute (KFRI, India) due to its aggressive invasive characteristics [13]. Emphasizing the ‘seed to weed approach’ and capitalizing on their abundance of phytochemicals, Pp leaves were chosen as the precursor for CDs synthesis and the fabrication of anticorrosive coatings for the first time [11,14,15]. Notwithstanding this, the principal challenge was obtaining solid Pp-derived hydrophilic carbon dots (PNCD, Pp leaf-derived N-doped CDs) as polymer matrix fillers. To address this, carbon dot particles were affixed onto silica nanoparticles, resulting in silica-loaded PNCD (SPNCD) filler particles, which constitute a novel facet of this investigation. These nano-solid functional fillers, SPNCD, were integrated into epoxy to develop a seamless, anticorrosive coating for MS, designated as ESD. While epoxy coatings are esteemed for their flexibility, adhesion, rapid drying, low volatile organic content, cost-effectiveness, and sustainability, the challenge of matrix porosity, which compromises durability due to corrosive ion ingress, was effectively mitigated through the incorporation of nanofillers [16,17]. In the realm of surface and interface research, the synergistic effects of silica and PNCD, as well as their interactions with the epoxy matrix in ESD coatings, will elucidate novel insights and superior anticorrosion performance. Cheng Zhu et al. posit that CDs within polymers exhibit a self-healing mechanism through various bonding interactions, including hydrogen bonding, covalent bonding, and van der Waals interactions, established both among the CDs and between the CDs and the polymer matrix [18]. The various functional groups enriched PNCD enhances the interfacial adhesion between the matrix and the filler, reducing voids or weak spots that could otherwise cause mechanical failure. The PNCD particles, distinguished by a substantial surface area, facilitate a homogeneous dispersion with reinforcing agents such as silica. The PNCDs adsorbed onto nano silica confer nanoconfinement to the epoxy polymer chains. The presence of these nano-reinforcements restricts the mobility of the polymer chains, reducing the free volume and enhancing the overall rigidity and tensile strength of the composite. In this context, silica particles serve as larger reinforcing agents, while the PNCDs offer supplementary nanoscale reinforcement. Thus, the paramount objective of this research was to develop and formulate an anticorrosive coating utilizing Pp-derived carbon dot-adsorbed silica nanoparticle fillers to mitigate marine corrosion of MS.

Tetraethyl ortho silicate (TEOS), aqueous ammonia, and NaCl were provided by E Merk. Ethanol, Pp leaf, and mild steel (MS) coupons (Comprised of 98.75 % Fe with trace amounts of Mn, C, P, and S elements) were used. The coating material comprises a matrix of modified bisphenol-A epoxy resin combined with a polyamidoimidazoline hardener [13].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nanofiller and fabrication of coating
ce:section.ce:para: Pp leaves were employed as a precursor for the hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (PNCDs). The leaves underwent a multi-step purification and processing procedure, including cleaning, drying, grinding, and storage. A homogeneous suspension of the powdered leaves in double-distilled water was prepared and subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 10 h in a Teflon-lined autoclave. The resulting PNCDs were isolated through a series of filtration, centrifugation, and dialysis steps to remove impurities and the purified product was stored at 4 °C for further use.

The precipitated nano silica adsorbent was prepared using the Stober method [19]. In this procedure, ammonia, ethanol, and water were combined to form a basic aqueous solution. Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), a silicon alkoxide, was added to the solution, initiating a hydrolysis and condensation reaction. The reaction mixture was stirred at 60 °C for 6 h, leading to the formation of a silica sol. The sol was then aged, centrifuged, and washed with ethanol to remove unreacted precursors and by-products. Finally, the silica sol was dried at 80 °C and ground in a mortar to convert it into a solid nano silica powder. The resulting nano silica was characterized and used in the coating formulation.

A novel and facile anticorrosive coating was fabricated using silica nanoparticles and carbon dots (PNCDs). Silica nanoparticles were dispersed in ethanol and mixed with various concentrations of PNCDs to create different compositions labelled SPNCD-25, SPNCD-50, SPNCD-75, and SPNCD-100. The mixtures were stirred and dried, resulting in a brown powder. PL analysis revealed that SPNCD-100, with the highest PNCD concentration, exhibited the highest fluorescence intensity, indicating its potential for enhanced anticorrosive properties.

Metal specimens were cleaned according to ASTM standards before the coating was applied. Briefly, metal parts were rubbed with 80, 180, 240, and 800-grit emery sheets, washed with distilled water, diluted HCl, ethanol, and acetone, and then dried. The silica nanofiller (ES) and SPNCD were blended with the epoxy coating formulation (ESD) (resin (2ml): hardener (1ml)); using a Specktom dip coater for dip coating. The coatings were then cured for 72 h, and their anticorrosive performance was compared.

The nanofiller particles (PNCD and SPNCD), impregnated films, and coated MS coupons before and after corrosion were characterized using routine techniques, as listed in Table S1. The gravimetric approach was considered appealing since it produced the most reliable outcomes without the need for any special equipment [20]. With this method, precisely mass-measured uncoated and coated MS panels were immersed in NaCl for 24 h to 96 h, after which they were removed, cleaned, dried, and weighed. The weight loss from the corrosion process was then compared to that of the bare metal. The weight loss values of bare (W 0) and coated metals (W) were used to compute PE (η) using the following formula:
(1)
η =
W 0
− W
W 0
× 100

Likewise, by calculating the weight loss (W), density (ρ), surface area (A), and exposure period (t) of a metal submerged in corrosive media, we were able to determine the corrosion rate.
(2)
C . R . =
k W
ρ A t

Where 'k' is a constant [20]. The electrochemical studies of the anticorrosive coating of the ESD coat in 3.5 wt% NaCl were conducted using a Gill AC (model no 1475) with a three-electrode cell system, in which a 10–4m2 open circular coated and uncoated metal pellet served as the working electrode, a saturated calomel electrode as the reference electrode, and a Pt electrode as the counter electrode. The 10 mV sine wave AC voltage was used to record the impedance spectra across a frequency range of 10–2 to 104 Hz. The PDP investigations were carried out at a sweep speed of 103 mVS-1, applying −250 to +250 mV. ZsimpWin software was used to record and extract the corrosion parameters of the MS coupons submerged in 3.5wt% NaCl for 1 hour to 60 days and to compare the effectiveness of the coated specimens. All corrosion studies were repeated at least three times to ensure the reproducibility of the data.

A bright-blue fluorescent hydrophilic CD was hydrothermally synthesized from tropical kudzu leaf extract in a one-pot reaction. Ogugor Juliet C et al. enlisted the phytochemicals of aqueous Pp leaf extract, including alkaloids, flavonoids, phlobatannins, saponins, steroids, tannins, terpenoids, carbohydrates, phytosterols, and phenolic components [21]. These biomolecules, containing nitrogen and oxygen heteroatoms, underwent regulated growth using the bottom-up methodology to yield non-toxic PNCD.

The elemental composition of SPNCD was verified through CHNS analysis. A comparison between SPNCD and pure silica nanoparticles was carried out by recording the IR spectrum (Fig. 3 A). Additional peaks emerged in SPNCD at 2924, 2854, and 1461 cm-1, corresponding to the CH symmetric, CH asymmetric, and CN stretching vibrations of the adsorbed PNCD particles. The broader peak at 3500–3100cm−1 corresponded to OH stretching vibration [32]. The peaks appeared at 1630, 1103, 474, cm−1 were associated with the OH , Si-O-Si, and Si-O-C bond vibrations, respectively [29]. No discernible peak shift was observed in the SPNCD compared to the PNCD, which suggests that the PNCD particles were merely adsorbed onto the silica spheres, with no strong chemical interactions existing between the adsorbent and adsorbate. As seen in the UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 3B.), the number of peaks increased from one to two in SPNCD compared to silica. The two SPNCD peaks were red-shifted slightly compared to PNCD, implying π-π interactions between the functionalized CD and silica nanoparticles. Therefore, the effective loading of the PNCDs onto nanosilica was confirmed by the vibrational and optical spectroscopic methods. The broadness, marginal increase in intensity, and almost identical XRD peak of SPNCD (figure S2A) demonstrated its amorphous nature and the effective addition of PNCDs onto silica [33–35]. The decomposition of functional groups of adsorbed PNCDs and the heat condensation of residual silanol groups were the causes of the increase in weight loss shown in the TGA curve (figure S2B) of SPNCD [36]. The sizes of approximately 100 silica and SPNCD nanospheres were measured from the FESEM images using ImageJ software, and the corresponding histograms were shown in figures S3A and S3B. The average diameters of unloaded and loaded nanoparticles were found to be 77.09 nm and 90.47 nm, respectively. The EDS measurement was also performed for compositional analysis of SPNCD nanoparticles, and the result is shown in figure S4. The higher percentage of O (49.93 %), the 6.83 atomic % of N, and the 22.82 % of C indicated that PNCDs were successfully adsorbed onto silica nanoparticles.
The thickness of the epoxy-filled SPNCD (ESD)-coated MS was measured using a profilometer and found to be 21 µm, as shown in figure S5.

The longevity and durability of ESD-coated MS were compared to bare metal over time, as shown in table S2. In the initial 24 h, the well applied ESD-coated MS in saline water exhibited 97.75 % PE. During this period, the corrosion rate was very low (1.3 × 10–5). PNCD, enriched with hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amine-containing functional groups enhanced the interfacial adhesion between the matrix and the filler, reducing voids or weak spots to resist the diffusion of corrosive ions to the MS surface. The weight loss analysis, which continued up to 96 h, showed that the ESD-coated MS experienced only a 0.0132 gm weight loss with 96.2 % PE.

The impedance spectroscopic technique successfully portrayed the protective action of the ESD coat on MS compared to pure epoxy and epoxy loaded with silica nanoparticles. The 0.2 % to 0.5 % ESD-coated specimens were immersed in saline solution and left undisturbed for one hour to obtain a constant rest potential value, after which their impedance spectra were recorded. Fig. 4 A shows the Nyquist plots of the bare MS, along with epoxy (EP), epoxy + silica (ES), epoxy + 0.2 %SPNCD (0.2ESD), epoxy + 0.3 %SPNCD (0.3ESD), epoxy + 0.4 %SPNCD (0.4ESD), and epoxy + 0.5 %SPNCD (0.5ESD) coated MS in a 3.5wt% NaCl solution at 25 °C. The sole approach to determining the anticorrosive behaviour of the coating was to fit the appropriate electrochemical equivalent cells to the Nyquist plot [37]. Accordingly, the circuit [R(Q(R(QR)))], which comprises two CPEs (Qc, Qdl) and three resistances (Rs, Rc, Rct), best fit the resulting Nyquist plots of the coated samples (Table 1 ). Solution resistance (Rs), coating capacitance (Qc), coating resistance to electrolyte passage (Rc), double layer capacitance (Qdl) between the electrolyte solution and the metal surface, and charge transfer resistance (Rct) across the metal surface are the components of the electrical circuit ‘B’ [38]. The R(QR) model was adopted to investigate the corrosion mechanism involved in the uncoated MS in the same corrosive media and the environmental conditions. The electrolyte resistance is constructed in series with the parallel arrangement of the CPE and Rct in R(QR) [39]. A high Rct value of 0.4ESD (5.85×104) indicates a strong anticorrosive barrier effect of SPNCD particles. As the SPNCD dose increases, the semi-circular diameter also increases, indicating that the Rct and Rc values are directly proportional to the nanofiller concentration. Using the following equation, the PE of the ESD and ES coatings is determined:
(3)
P E
( % )
=
R
c t
*
−
R
c t
R
c t
× 100

The 30th and 60th day EIS studies introduced an additional resistance factor, that is Warburg impedance (WR). Consequently, the PE of epoxy and ESD coatings can also be computed in terms of polarization resistance (Rp). If the fitted circuit contains only one resistance (Rct), Rp is equal to Rct. If there is more than one resistance in the circuit, Rp is the sum of all resistances other than Rs [40,41].
(4)
R p
=
R c
+
R
c t
+
W
R

To calculate the PE based on Rp, the following equation is used:
(5)
P E
( % )
=
R
p , C
−
R
p , B
R
p , C
× 100
Where ‘Rp,C’ is the polarization resistance of coated MS and ‘Rp,B’ for polarisation resistance of bare MS.

In Eq. (3),
R
c t
*
corresponds to the coated species, whereas Rct corresponds to the bare metal. When comparing bare samples with coated one,
R
c t
*
exhibits significant shift in value [13]. The thin layer of 0.2ESD coat at the metal/solution interface yielded a higher Rct (6264Ω.cm2) that was 20 times higher than that of bare MS (340Ω.cm2). Meanwhile, Qdl values exhibited an inverse proportionality to the concentration of ESD filler [42]. The emergence of the Rct and Qdl elements indicated that the saline media had reached the MS surface through the pores in epoxy. By lowering porosity, nanohybrid fillers provide a consistent layer of protection for the metal surface. Due to the typical flaws in epoxy coatings, oxygen and corrosive agents can easily pass through the coating and reach the metal substrate surface (Rc = 330.1 Ω.cm²), leading to significant corrosion. Although the penetration of corrosive media (Rc=400.21 Ω.cm2) was limited by ES coating, the presence of hydrogen bonds on the silanol groups and large surface area together contributes to nanoparticle agglomeration (PE of ES =85.45). PNCD adsorption on silica increases compaction by reducing the porosity of the epoxy coating (Rc=752.5 Ω.cm2). According to the corrosion study, the PE of SPNCD in the epoxy matrix increases with an increase in filler concentration, as shown by the following order: 0.2ESD (94.57) < 0.3ESD (99.15) < 0.4ESD (99.42) > 0.5ESD (97.34). A concentration of <0.2wt% may not provide sufficient barrier properties, while concentrations greater than 0.4wt% can lead to particle agglomeration through filler-filler interaction. This will result in poor dispersion and reduced adhesion to the substrate. The 0.4wt% concentration, as optimal level, creates a tortuous path for corrosive agents (Rc= 752.5 Ω.cm2), effectively slowing their penetration. Concentrations greater than 0.4wt% SPNCD added to the epoxy matrix could exceed the percolation threshold. This means the inorganic-organic hybrid composite reaches a critical point at which the material transitions from being non-permeable to permeable. At this point, the composite material undergoes a phase transition from an insulating to a conducting state. At this stage, the SPNCD particles form clusters through filler-filler interactions, increasing the porosity of the coating and allowing the flow of electrolyte through the system. This leads to a decrease in the value of Rct (0.5ESD = 1.28×104 Ω.cm2). The electron-rich functional moieties on the SPNCD spheres can interact with the MS by donating electrons to the vacant d orbitals of Fe particles, thereby enhancing the adhesion of the coating material to the metal specimen. Consequently, an increased concentration of filler particles results in a more stable coating material.

The PNCD particles present on the coating surface interacted with corrosive ions, preventing their penetration through the coating. The higher Rct value of 0.4ESD compared to 0.4ES (2336 Ω.cm2) in Fig. 4B showed that PNCD played a prominent role in the protective action of the coating matrix. Silica nanoparticles were selected due to their compatibility with the epoxy matrix and strong adsorption properties. The high values of Rct and Rc and the low values of Qc and Qdl of ESD supported the involvement of PNCD in the barrier action against corrosion [43].

The long-term immersion analysis, as reflected in the Bode plot, elucidated the barrier mechanism responsible for the higher Rct of the 0.4ESD coating. It was noted that the initially high Rct at 24-hours marginally decreased and then stabilized at a constant value over the next two months (9819 Ω.cm2). The permeation of electrolytic ions into the coating-substrate interface during the initial hour was responsible for the lower Rc and higher Qc (Table 2 ). Within 24 h, the PNCD particles adsorbed onto the MS surface and formed a robust, uniform protective barrier that shielded against the diffused ions. Environmental conditions promoted the desorption of adsorbate molecules over time, allowing corrosive ions to penetrate the MS surface and degrade the substrate. The Nyquist plots from the one-month and two-month immersion studies were fitted with a suitable electrical equivalent circuit, R(Q(R(Q(RW)))) as shown in Fig. 4E [44,45]. On the 30th day, the 0.4ESD coating likely maintained its integrity and effectively impeded ion penetration. This resulted in high ‘W’ and low ‘Qdl’ values compared to the 60th day EIS study (Table 2). When the diffusion process dominated, it contributed to capacitive characteristics, as the system accumulated charges in proportion to the applied potential over time. The barrier activity of the PNCD particles and any corrosion products on the ESD coating restricted the formation of the double layer at the electrode/ electrolyte interface. This suggested that fewer ions were able to reach and interact with the surface, limiting charge accumulation. The Warburg impedance (Wimp) represented the barrier effect of the coating against the diffusion of the electrolyte. Specifically, Warburg impedance became significant when a diffusion–controlled process occurred in the system. On day 30, WR (7.86× 1011) displayed a higher value, but on day 60, it reached its lowest value (9.89× 10–5). This was because there had been no noticeable corrosion or corrosion products for up to 30 days. Afterward, the rate of corrosion slightly increased due to the diffusion of corrosive ions to the metal substrate, leading to the formation of corrosion products on the coating's surface. These products obstructed the coating's diffusion channel, resulting in an increase in Wimp. Over prolonged immersion, the lower W after 60 days implied that the desorption of the PNCD particles from the silica surface chemically altered the coating, making it more porous. This enhanced the penetration of water and chloride ions from the NaCl solution, leading to the formation of micro-defects in the coating. This thereby reduced the effectiveness of the protective barrier and altered ion transport pathways. The formation of micro channels within the coating facilitated easier ion movement, leading to a drop in W. This resulted in increased charge storage and a higher Qdl value. In contrast, the lower Qdl on the 30th day suggested that the coating was still effectively inhibiting ion movement, indicating that diffusion was more controlled and restricted [46].

The superior barrier function and remarkable longevity of the ESD coating, compared to typical epoxy coatings, were demonstrated in the Bode phase angle diagram (Fig. 4D). The two-month analysis of the high- and medium-frequency capacitive loops of the coated MS specimens revealed the maximum phase value of ESD. The phase angle of the epoxy coating decreased in the low-frequency area relative to the ESD coating. Additionally, the epoxy coating exhibited a peak in the mid-high frequency region at the same frequency as the mid-low frequency region in the ESD study. The reduced phase angle in the low-frequency region indicated a high corrosion rate. The second time constant, in this case, corresponded to the mid-low and high-frequency peak [47]. In comparison to the first stage, the emergence of the second time constant showed an enhanced corrosion response area at the coating/substrate interface.

The protective action of the ESD coating against corrosion was investigated using the polarisation method. The protection efficiency was calculated from the icorr values using the following equation;
(6)
η PDP
=
I
corr
*
−
I corr
I
corr
*
× 100

Where
I
corr
*
is the corrosion current density of the unshielded and icorr is the corrosion current density of the shielded MS samples [48]. The anodic and cathodic slopes also help determine the linear polarization resistance (Rp) of the coating, which indicates the barrier action of the coating against corrosive ions. The following equation was used to calculate the Rp value:
(7)
L P R ,
R p
=
β
a
β c
2.303
(
β a
+
β c
)
i
c o r r

The anodic and cathodic current densities were lowered in the presence of PNCD, resulting in a comparable decrease in the anodic dissolution and cathodic reduction processes, as shown in Fig. 5 B [49]. Generally, the formation of ferrous ions with the ejection of electrons is called the anodic reaction, whereas the reduction of oxygen and water molecules occurs in the cathodic part. Tables 3 and 4 summarizes the PDP parameters derived from the Tafel plot of the uncoated and coated metal pellets. The icorr values of the bare metal and epoxy-coated MS were found to be 0.1331mA/cm2 and 0.0199mA/cm2, respectively. The icorr value was reduced by 100 times when the MS was covered with ESD (10–4mA/cm2). Fig. 5A depicts that the protection efficiency increases as the filler dose increases. The PE was 93.39 % for 0.2ESD, rising to 99.17 % for 0.4ESD, and then decreasing to 85.8 % with 0.5ESD. Beyond the optimum ESD, the filler–filler interactions generate an uneven surface, which reduces the effectiveness of protection. The more positive Ecorr, higher Rp, and smaller icorr values of 0.4ESD MS explain its high barrier action through excellent surface modification of the epoxy coating. The optimum concentration of nanofiller in the coating matrix offered better protection, while also providing double protection to the MS substrate by donating lone pairs from heteroatoms (nitrogen and oxygen atoms) and functional groups (hydroxyl and carboxyl) on PNCD to the vacant d orbital of the metal atom. This protective surface blocked the penetration of chloride-like corrosive ions to the metal surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and optical characteristics of 0.4ESD film
ce:section.ce:para: The typical 2D and 3D AFM images of the 0.4ESD MS specimens, before and after the corrosion study, are shown in Fig. 10 . The 2D image of the coating before immersion in the aggressive media portrays the homogenously dispersed nanofillers in the epoxy matrix. The average height of the surface particles indicated a particle size of 4 nm, suggesting that the AFM technique detected the presence of PNCD on the ESD surface [50]. The surface roughness, assessed using Ra (average surface roughness) and Rq (root-mean-square surface roughness), was 0.395 nm and 0.517 nm, respectively, prior to exposure to corrosive media [51]. After 24 h, the surface roughness values were estimated to be 0.470 nm and 0.635 nm on the Ra and Rq scales, respectively. This indicates that when exposed to the hostile environment, the ESD coating is not severely damaged, and the 3D images confirm this conclusion. Even after immersion, the surface's homogeneity was maintained. Therefore, the newly synthesized ESD coating is a robust and compact anticorrosive coating for MS in saline conditions.

Harnessing the green strategy, PNCDs were synthesized from the leaves of Pueraria phaseoloides. FTIR and UV-visible spectroscopy were used to identify the main surface-active functional groups in PNCD that are responsible for the anticorrosive action. Furthermore, freshly synthesized silica nanoparticles facilitated the transformation of liquid PNCD into powder SPNCD, which was characterized using routine TGA, FTIR, UV-visible, XRD, and SEM techniques. The gravimetric study testified that the rate of attack by corrosive ions is minimal (3.8 × 10–5) in ESD-coated MS after 96 h of immersion in saline media. Environmentally benign and economically viable SPNCD nanofillers were engrafted into the epoxy coating (0.4ESD), resulting in exceptional corrosion resistance tailored for marine applications, with 99.42 % efficiency. The superior barrier performance was owed to the inherent dynamic and active corrosion resistance of SPNCD. The performance and durability of the nano-hybrid coating were monitored and compared with ES and bare epoxy coatings under the same conditions using EIS and PDP techniques. By increasing the concentration from 0.2 to 0.4wt%, the appropriately adsorbed fillers enhance the diffusion path length for corrosive agents, thereby decreasing the Qdl value. The filler-filler interaction is accountable for the lower P.E. at a higher dose of ESD (0.5ESD = 1.28×104 Ω.cm2). This phenomenon leads to the breakdown of the coating's compactness, which in turn creates an unstable coating. Further, the surface morphology investigations of an ESD-coated metal specimen before and after the corrosion investigation indicate that SPNCD serves as a propitious nanofiller, addressing the issue of matrix porosity by blocking the penetration of corrosive ions and providing a seamless anticorrosive coating. The well-dispersed and uniformly distributed 0.4wt% SPNCD particles within the epoxy matrix create a more uniform barrier, enhancing the coating's anticorrosion ability. The green nanocomposite coating derived from an invasive weed serves as a promising sustainable anticorrosive contender in maritime applications.

Thank you very much for reconsidering my paper for to be published in this prestigious journal. Herewith I am submitting the Revised paper entitled Fossicking Pueraria phraseologies leaf-derived N-doped carbon dot nanofillers engrafted anticorrosive epoxy coatings for mild steel in saline environments for your kind consideration. There is absolutely no conflict of interest exist regarding this work and all authors checked the content of the paper and authors do assure that if this paper is accepted for publication in Journal of Surfaces and Interfaces its content will not be published in any languages, in the same form without the consent of the publisher. Also state that this paper has not been given to any other journal for consideration to be published.

Anila Paul: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Muhammed Arshad: Validation, Software, Formal analysis. Athira Ajayan: Validation, Methodology, Formal analysis. Abraham Joseph: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the support provided by the University of Calicut for conducting the research at the Department of Chemistry. The research was supported by technical assistance from CSIF-University of Calicut, STICCUSAT Kerala, DST- PURSE Facility, Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut and KSCSTE Facility at Environmental and Sustainable Chemistry Laboratory (ESCL), Department of Chemistry, University of Calicut.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.surfin.2025.105784.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix
ce:section.ce:para: Fig.S1. Structural characterisation of PNCD, (A) vibrational spectroscopy discloses the picture of surface-active functional groups on the carbon quantum dot, (B) figure out the nature of the graphitic core through Raman spectra.
Fig.S2. (A) XRD pattern of silica compared with PNCD, and SPNCD, (B) TGA curves of silica and SPNCD powder recorded by applying temperatures from 20 to 1200 °c.

Fig.S3. SEM images of pure silica nano powder and brown SPNCD powder. (A) and (B) depicts the histograms of SPNCD powder, and the EDS spectra in the following figure shows the adsorption of PNCD on silica.
Fig.S4. The EDS spectra show the adsorption of PNCD on silica.
Fig.S5. Profilometer diagram of the ESD film on MS.
Table.S1. A summary of the utilized instruments and their specifications.

Table.S2. Weight loss analysis of bare MS compared with 0.4ESD for 24 to 96 h in saline media.
Image, application 1
